coredata.dc:title: Tunable fluorescent carbon dots from biowaste as fluorescence ink and imaging human normal and cancer cells
coredata.dc:creator: Atchudan, Raji
Chandra Kishore, Somasundaram
Gangadaran, Prakash
Jebakumar Immanuel Edison, Thomas Nesakumar
Perumal, Suguna
Rajendran, Ramya Lakshmi
Alagan, Muthulakshmi
Al-Rashed, Sarah
Ahn, Byeong-Cheol
Lee, Yong Rok
coredata.dc:description: Growing global biowaste and its environmental issues challenge the need for converting biowastes into a beneficial product. Among the biowaste, here kiwi fruit (Actinidia Deliciosa) peels are considered for the preparation of carbon dots (CDs). Using a green one-pot hydrothermal-carbonization method, kiwi fruit peels were effectively converted into valuable kiwi fruit peel carbon dots (KFP-CDs). The morphology, physio-chemical and optical properties of as-synthesized KFP-CDs were analyzed using various analytical techniques such as X-ray powder diffraction, Raman spectroscopy, attenuated total reflection-Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, field emission scanning electron microscopy, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, Ultraviolet–visible, and fluorescence spectroscopy. The KFP-CDs revealed a homogeneous spherical shape, monodispersed with an average size of 5 nm. The characterization confirms that KFP-CDs have functional groups such as –CN, –COOH, and –OH which are responsible for the easy dispersion of KFP-CDs in aqueous media. Without any preprocessing, KFP-CDs exhibit strong fluorescence upon exposure to UV light. Further, KFP-CDs displayed excitation-dependent fluorescence emission with a good quantum yield of about 18%. Thus by considering the excellent properties of KFP-CDs, KFP-CDs were used as fluorescent ink for drawing and writing without any capping/passivation agent. The pictures and words were instantaneously viewed when exposed to UV light. In addition, KFP-CDs tested for cell imaging in four human cell lines (normal and cancer cells) bestowed excellent biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity, which is important for the safe and long-term development of cellular imaging. The findings imply that KFP-CDs can be utilized as a cell labeling agent for mesenchymal stem cells, breast cancer, and thyroid cancer cells in vitro imaging. Thus, these observations revealed that investigating sustainable resource-based CDs can open up new avenues for tackling environmental issues.

head.ce:title: Tunable fluorescent carbon dots from biowaste as fluorescence ink and imaging human normal and cancer cells

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Growing global biowaste and its environmental issues challenge the need for converting biowastes into a beneficial product. Among the biowaste, here kiwi fruit (Actinidia Deliciosa) peels are considered for the preparation of carbon dots (CDs). Using a green one-pot hydrothermal-carbonization method, kiwi fruit peels were effectively converted into valuable kiwi fruit peel carbon dots (KFP-CDs). The morphology, physio-chemical and optical properties of as-synthesized KFP-CDs were analyzed using various analytical techniques such as X-ray powder diffraction, Raman spectroscopy, attenuated total reflection-Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, field emission scanning electron microscopy, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, Ultraviolet–visible, and fluorescence spectroscopy. The KFP-CDs revealed a homogeneous spherical shape, monodispersed with an average size of 5 nm. The characterization confirms that KFP-CDs have functional groups such as –CN, –COOH, and –OH which are responsible for the easy dispersion of KFP-CDs in aqueous media. Without any preprocessing, KFP-CDs exhibit strong fluorescence upon exposure to UV light. Further, KFP-CDs displayed excitation-dependent fluorescence emission with a good quantum yield of about 18%. Thus by considering the excellent properties of KFP-CDs, KFP-CDs were used as fluorescent ink for drawing and writing without any capping/passivation agent. The pictures and words were instantaneously viewed when exposed to UV light. In addition, KFP-CDs tested for cell imaging in four human cell lines (normal and cancer cells) bestowed excellent biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity, which is important for the safe and long-term development of cellular imaging. The findings imply that KFP-CDs can be utilized as a cell labeling agent for mesenchymal stem cells, breast cancer, and thyroid cancer cells in vitro imaging. Thus, these observations revealed that investigating sustainable resource-based CDs can open up new avenues for tackling environmental issues.

ce:section.ce:para: Rising worldwide pollution problems at an increasing rate are causing severe everlasting harm to the environment and human mankind (Naidu et al., 2021; Yadav et al., 2021). Around one-third of all food grown is lost as waste, leading to financial loss and causing a threat to the environment and is growing on a daily basis, providing a management and disposal challenge (Yahya et al., 2015). Nevertheless, the majority of biomass waste is now dumped, disposed of, or openly burnt, resulting in resource loss as well as environmental issues that endanger human health (Kaur and Sarao, 2021). Now there is an urgent need to resolve these environmental concerns, such as how to properly utilize this trash as an effective raw material with a reliable beneficial artifact.

To achieve this long-term goal, the major goal has been to develop and synthesize innovative materials in a more environmentally friendly manner using renewable and accessible resources. During the last three decades, nanocarbon (NC) materials have been the focus of considerable study (Sabzehmeidani et al., 2021). For various physical and chemical uses, a range of NCs, such as graphene (Reiss et al., 2019), carbon nanotubes (De Volder et al., 2013), carbon nanodots (Ostadhossein and Pan, 2017), carbon nanofibers (Raji et al., 2021), fullerenes (Puente Santiago et al., 2021) and nanodiamonds (Vervald et al., 2021), are nearing or have already achieved the marketing phase. Carbon dots (CDs) are one of the most significant members of the NC group because of their exceptional fluorescence properties (Perumal et al., 2021). CDs are spherical particles with a size of less than 10 nm. CDs are widely distributed with several attractive features, including excellent photostability, lower toxicity, good biocompatibility, simple surface functionalization, and greater chemical stability, in addition to the high quantum yield (QY) and adjustable emission wavelength that conventional semiconductor quantum dots have (Park et al., 2014; Xiao and Sun, 2018). Because of their remarkable structural and optical characteristics, CDs have been suggested as the best candidate for fluorescent ink (Atchudan et al., 2018d; Kumar et al., 2021), drug delivery (Wang et al., 2016b), catalysts (Liu et al., 2016), sensor (Wu et al., 2017) and light-emitting diodes (Xie et al., 2017). As a result, they have received intensive scientific considerable interest.

The synthesis method of CDs mainly includes two approaches such as top-down (laser ablation (Xu et al., 2017), arc discharge (Nevar et al., 2021) and electrochemical synthesis (Wang et al., 2014)) and bottom-up (ultrasonic method (Manoharan et al., 2020), pyrolysis (Ortega-Liebana et al., 2017), microwave-assisted method (Zhai et al., 2012), solvothermal method (Wang et al., 2016a)). The primary materials used to produce CDs are numerous and may be divided into two categories such as inorganic and organic carbon precursors. Inorganic precursors yield CDs with low QY and need surface passivation for enhancing the same (Li and Dong, 2018). As a result, organic carbon resources including organic chemicals, organic natural products, and biomass trashes are more commonly employed in the production of CDs in practice (Khairol Anuar et al., 2021; Perumal et al., 2021).

Biomass sources used for CDs production possess complicated physical and chemical properties resulting in heterogeneous properties after carbonization (Cheng et al., 2017). There are numerous reports on CDs derived from a wide range of sources, including grass, organic household waste, agricultural, fisheries, livestock, forestry, and associated industrial leftovers (Huang et al., 2019; Perumal et al., 2021). Biomass waste is mainly composed of proteins, cellulose, lignin, hemicellulose, ash, and other components (Zong et al., 2020). Biomass combustion is a process that encompasses a variety of thermochemical conversion methods. The most common methods used in biomass carbonization include the hydrothermal method (Hu et al., 2010) and pyrolysis (slow/fast/mild) (Liu and Balasubramanian, 2014).

Carbon nanomaterials doped with heteroatoms often increase their inherent characteristics and substantially broaden their applicability in the area of electrochemical, photocatalytic, bioimaging, and sensing (Atchudan et al., 2016; Edison et al., 2016a). There are many reports on the production of heteroatom-doped CDs with good photoluminescence characteristics. Bioimaging is essential for monitoring in vivo distribution of cells and targeting diseases such as cancer, ischemic diseases, and inflammatory diseases (Wolfbeis, 2015). Bioimaging has contributed to the development of cell-based therapies against various diseases through preclinical and clinical studies, bioimaging application will eventually reduce the cost and time required for the development of cell-based therapies (Gangadaran and Ahn, 2017; Gangadaran et al., 2020; Lee et al., 2016; Rajendran et al., 2020). CDs have a broad array of applications throughout the field of bioimaging due to their outstanding optical characteristics (Atchudan et al., 2018c, 2019a; Chen et al., 2013; Edison et al., 2020). Furthermore, CDs have minimal cytotoxicity and high biocompatibility, making them ideal for in vivo imaging of cells and tissues (Li et al., 2010).

Here in this study, we use the hydrothermal aided carbonization method to synthesize CDs from kiwi fruit peel (Actinidia Deliciosa). Kiwi fruit feel is easily available and affordable common food waste. Polyphenols, carbohydrates, minerals, vitamin C, dietary fiber, chlorophylls, carotenoids, and antioxidants are rich in kiwi fruit peel (Sanz et al., 2021). Thus, the kiwi fruit peel was consumed as a carbon source for the synthesis of durable fluorescence KFP-CDs. The resulting CDs were denoted as KFP-CDs and their structural (X-ray powder diffraction (XRD), Raman spectroscopy, attenuated total reflection-Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR), field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS)) and optical (Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorbance and fluorescence) properties were investigated thoroughly. The synthesized KFP-CDs were used as fluorescent ink for drawing and writing without any capping/passivation agent. Further, a Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK8) test was used to assess the impact of synthesized KFP-CDs on the cellular viability of human normal and cancer cells. KFP-CDs were employed as a fluorescent agent for laser confocal microscopy imaging of bone-marrow derived mesenchymal stem cells (BM-MSCs), M.D. Anderson - Metastatic Breast 231 (MDA-MB-231, breast cancer cells), Centre Antoine Lacassagne-62 (Cal62, thyroid cancer cells) and BHP10-3SCp (thyroid cancer cells) to confirm the potential applicability of synthesized KFP-CDs for live-cell imaging. To the best of the authors' knowledge, this is the earliest report on the BM-MSCs, MDA-MB-231, Cal62, and BHP10-3SCp cellular imaging with good biocompatibility and fluorescence ink for writing and drawing patterns with durability using kiwi fruit feel waste-derived KFP-CDs.

The KFP-CDs were synthesized from kiwi fruit peel (KFP) via a one-step hydrothermal route. In detail, the kiwi fruit peel was crushed well with deionized water using a commercial mixer grinder. The obtained mucilage (∼70 mL) and aqueous ammonia (∼1 mL) were collected in a 100 mL capacity Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave (pH ∼8) and then heated at 200 °C for 24 h. After the reaction time, the autoclave was allowed to cool to room temperature. The hydrothermally carbonized suspension was filtered using a cellulose membrane with a pore size of 0.22 μm to remove the bigger carbon particles. The resultant yellowish-brown colored supernatant (KFP-CDs) was transferred to a glass vial and stored in a refrigerator at 5 °C for further studies. Scheme 1 illustrates the diagram of the detailed synthesis of KFP-CDs from the KFP.

The human BM-MSCs were purchased from American Type Culture Collection (Manassas, VA, USA). The human breast cancer cells line (MDA-MB-231) was purchased from the Korean cell line bank, Seoul. Human thyroid cancer cell lines (Cal62) were purchased from Deutsche Sammlung von Mikroorganismen and Zellkulturen (DSMZ; Braunschweig, Germany), and human thyroid cancer cell line (BHP10-3SCp) was generously gifted by Prof. Dr. Young Joo Park, professor in Seoul National University College of Medicine.

BM-MSCs cells were maintained in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium/F-12 (HyClone, Logan, UT, USA); MDA-MB-231 and Cal62 cells were maintained in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (HyClone, Logan, UT, USA). BHP10-3SCp was maintained in Roswell Park Memorial Institute (RPMI) 1640 medium (HyClone, Logan, UT, USA). All the mediums were supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) (HyClone, Logan, UT, USA) and 1% penicillin-streptomycin (Gibco, Carlsbad, CA, USA) at 37 °C with 5% CO2.

BM-MSCs, MDA-MB-231, Cal62, and BHP10-3SCp (1 × 104/well) were seeded into a 96-well plate in 100 μL of complete medium for overnight under 5% CO2 atmosphere at 37 °C. Cells were treated with various concentration of KFP-CDs (0, 25, 50, 75, 100, 125, 150, 175 and 200 μg mL−1) for 24 and 48 h. At 24 and 48 h, 10 μL of CCK8 solution was added to each well. Cells were incubated under a 5% CO2 atmosphere at 37 °C for 2 h. Subsequently, optical density (OD) was measured on an enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay plate reader according to the manufacturer's protocol.

BM-MSCs, MDA-MB-231, Cal62, and BHP10-3SCp (5 × 104/well) were seeded into 8 well slide-chamber in 1 mL of complete medium for overnight under 5% CO2 atmosphere at 37 °C. Cells were treated KFP-CDs (150 μg mL−1) for 24 and 48 h. After then, cells were washed twice with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) for 10 min and fixed with 2% paraformaldehyde for 10 min at room temperature. Then, cells were washed twice with PBS for 10 min. Slides were mounted with an antifade mounting medium (Vectashield, CA, USA). Cells were imaged under the highly sensitive super-resolution confocal laser scanning microscope LSM 800 (Carl Zeiss, Baden-Württemberg, Germany).

In order to study the crystalline nature of the KFP-CDs, the samples were subjected to X-ray diffraction and Raman spectroscopy analysis. Fig. 1 (a) shows the XRD pattern pertaining to KFP-CDs. The peaks formed at (2θ = ) 22.7 and 42.5°corresponds to the (002) and (100) planes, authenticating peaks of carbon materials (Atchudan et al., 2019b; Hsu et al., 2012). It is observed that the intensity of the peaks is very weak and broad, indicating the amorphous nature/moderate graphitic nature of carbon. Using the (002) plane, the interlayer spacing of the KFP-CDs was determined to be 0.39 nm. Furthermore, the existence of extensive functional groups at the edges of the KFP-CDs accounts for the larger interlayer spacing (0.39 nm) of the KFP-CDs in contrast to bulk graphite (0.34 nm). The larger interlayer spacing of KFP-CDs than bulk graphite might be ascribed to the minor expansion between carbon/graphene layers. In addition, this further could attribute to the deformation in the carbon lattice plane because of the heteroatom doping which induces structural defect (graphitic disorder) in KFP-CDs. The Raman spectrum of KFP-CDs is shown in Fig. 1(b). The spectrum showed two peaks at 1390 and 1590 cm−1 representing the D-band (sp3 carbon) and G-band (sp2 carbon) respectively (Atchudan et al., 2021b; Qu et al., 2012). The edge defects on the KFP-CDs could be attributed to the presence of the D-band, whereas G-band reveals the graphitic nature of the same. In addition, the two peaks are found to be overlapped in the middle region. Consequently, for better analysis, the spectrum is convoluted to two distinct peaks as shown in Fig. 1(c), where the peak area ratio (AD/AG) was calculated to be 1.7. This observation confirms the existence of amorphous/poor crystalline carbon, which coincides well with the XRD result. Presumably, the formation of amorphous carbon could account for the various functional group attached to edges of KFP-CDs.

FESEM analysis was conducted to investigate the morphology of the as-synthesized KFP-CDs. Figure S1 shows the FESEM images of KFP-CDs taken at various magnifications. KFP-CDs portray a smooth surface, which might be attributed to monodispersed clumping of the tiny particles. Furthermore, the EDX spectrum carried out to estimate the elemental composition, depicted the peaks associated with carbon, oxygen, nitrogen, and platinum. The source of carbon is confirmed from KFP; whereas the oxygen and nitrogen originate from the functional groups attached to the surface of KFP-CDs. The presence of platinum is due to the ohmic contact coating applied over the samples during the analysis. For better visualization and a better understanding of chemical composition, Fig. 2 illustrates the micrographs of the KFP-CDs with elemental mapping. It was interesting to note that various elements such as carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen were uniformly distributed over the KFP-CDs, corroborated by green-, red- and yellow-colored dots respectively on the micrographs. Noteworthy, the amount of carbon is seen dominating while compared to other elements. However, the nanosized KFP-CDs were found aggregated and formulated into a single carbon microparticle. When carbon dots with hygroscopic behavior were placed over silicon wafer during the FESEM analysis for drying get agglomerated into a few microparticles. This might happen due to the presence of various hydrophilic functional groups present over the surface of KFP-CDs.

The crystallinity and particle size of the KFP-CDs were evaluated using TEM analysis. Fig. 3 depicts the TEM and HRTEM images of the KFP-CDs. The image in Fig. 3(a) depicts monodispersed spherical KFP-CDs particle and the corresponding statistical histogram of particle size with an average size of 5 nm. Moreover, the HRTEM images divulge the existence of lattice fringes, with a d-spacing value of 0.21 nm, corresponding to C (100) graphitic plane of carbon in KFP-CDs (Edison et al., 2016a; Mattevi et al., 2009). Overall, these findings are in accordance with XRD and Raman spectroscopy which assess the moderate graphitic nature in KFP-CDs. Due to their smaller particle size, KFP-CDs may pass through the cell more freely by endocytosis than conventional semiconductor quantum dots or other nanoparticles, and thereby have broader use in cell imaging and measurement of tiny molecules in cells.

The functional groups and the bonding composition present over KFP-CDs were studied using ATR-FTIR spectroscopy. Figure S2 shows the ATR-FTIR spectrum of the as-synthesized KFP-CDs. The broad peak at 3200-3400 cm−1 is attributed to –O–H and –N–H aminoacidic group bonding vibration (Kumari et al., 2009). The peak at 620, 2929, and 2866 cm−1 is ascribed to C–H out of plane aromatic bond, –CH/–CH2 asymmetric and symmetric stretching aliphatic groups, respectively (Atchudan et al., 2019b; Đorđević et al., 2020). The peaks at 1070, 1275, 1392, 1456, 1585, and 1675 cm−1 are assigned to the functional groups C–O–C polysaccharide, C–O–H, –O–H bending, C–N amide II band, CC unsaturated aromatic compounds, and –CO carboxyl/amide I band, respectively (Atchudan et al., 2021a; Đorđević et al., 2020). The existence of C–OH and C–O–C groups are indicated by absorption bands about 1275 and 1070 cm−1 (Atchudan et al., 2016). Consequently, the inclusion of acid, hydroxyl, amino, and carbonyl functionalities on the KFP-CDs was verified by ATR-FTIR. KFP-CDs passivated or functionalized can increase their fluorescence emission intensity and hence their relevance to bioanalytical tests. Also, surface passivated KFP-CDs can reduce surface imperfections, increase exciton-hole recombination possibility, and boost fluorescence emission intensity. Furthermore, it is evident that carbonization and passivation coexist during the synthesis process of KFP-CDs from kiwi fruit peel waste due to the presence of long-chain carboxylic acids and other functional chemicals in carbon sources. As a consequence, KFP-CDs made from kiwi fruit peel waste are self-passivated. Passivation of the surface KFP-CDs adding amino, hydroxyl, and carboxyl functional groups can increase their water solubility, laying the groundwork for KFP-CDs to be used for cell imaging and fluorescent ink.

The atomic bonding, elemental composition, and the functional groups attached to the surface of synthesized KFP-CDs were assessed with XPS. Fig. 4 (a) shows the XPS survey spectrum of synthesized KFP-CDs confirming the presence of carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen species. The high-resolution XPS spectrum was further analyzed to determine the binding energy (BE) of the corresponding elements present. The XPS spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 4(b)) was deconvoluted into four sub-peaks at 284.7, 286.0, 287.2, and 288.7 eV representing CC/C–C, C–O/C–N/C–O–C, CO/CN and –COOH peaks, respectively (Atchudan et al., 2019a; Chen et al., 2020). The deconvoluted N 1s peak (Fig. 4(c)) is further fitted into three peaks with BE attributing pyridinic-N (C–N–C) at 399.1 eV, pyrrolic-N (C–N–H) at 400.2 eV, and graphitic-N ((C)3–N) at 401.6 eV (Atchudan et al., 2019a; Morant et al., 2006). The addition of heteroatoms to the carbon atom framework may efficiently enhance the electrical characteristics, intrinsic chemical properties, and surface chemical characteristics of KFP-CDs. The ease of nitrogen atom doped into KFP-CDs is because of its comparable atomic size to a carbon atom, five outermost orbital electrons, and greater electronegativity than carbon. It is noted from Fig. 4(d), O1s level of KFP-CDs can be deconvoluted into three fitting Gaussian peaks at 531.4, 532.6 and 533.4 eV imply the occurrence of typical CO, C–OH/C–O–C, and OC–OH bonds, respectively (Atchudan et al., 2019a; Yumitori, 2000). These findings well confirm the fact that KFP-CDs encompass the functionalities such as –C–N, –COOH, and –OH, etc., that coincide well with the observation obtained from ATR-FTIR. Consequently, the good dispersion and solubility of KFP-CDs are substantiated due to the presence of these functional groups on their surface.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum of KFP-CDs is shown in Fig. 5 (a). The spectrum indicates two prominent peaks at 274 and 322 nm that attribute to π–π* transition of aromatic CC bond and n–π* transition of CO/CN bonds present on the surface of KFP-CDs (Atchudan et al., 2020; Yan et al., 2020). This observation well coincides with the ATR-FTIR observations, confirming the fact that oxygen and nitrogen functional groups are present over the KFP-CDs surfaces. The fluorescence excitation spectrum of synthesized KFP-CDs is shown in Fig. 5(b). From the spectrum, a maximum excitation wavelength of 360 nm was obtained with a high intense peak denoting a bright fluorescence with KFP-CDs. Fluorescence emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths were analyzed as shown in Fig. 5(c). Interestingly, the intensity of the peak increases from 300 to 360 nm and gradually decreases upon increasing the excitation wavelength beyond 360–460 nm. Thus, maximum fluorescence intensity was obtained with an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. Moreover, a redshift is observed in fluorescence emission on increasing the excitation wavelength as shown in Fig. 5(d). Upon applying the excitation at 300 nm delivered the emission spectra at 400 nm, whereas excitation at 460 nm gives an emission at 500 nm. The QY of the synthesized KFP-CDs was calculated as 18% using quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 as a standard reference (Equation S1). Table 1 shows the comparison of quantum yield and excitation wavelength of CDs synthesized from various green precursors and methods. From the observations, our work produced CDs with a moderate better quantum yield using an economically simple precursor and method. Several parameters such as particle sizes, surface functional groups, and heteroatom doping are presumed to be the reason behind the fluorescence emissions mechanism (Zhu et al., 2015). Among them, the energy levels of the ligands attached to KFP-CDs tend to vary, which might produce a sequence of emissive traps that plays a major role. Thus, when the KFP-CDs are illuminated with UV light of a specific excitation wavelength, the emission is dominated by these surface state emissive traps. Furthermore, a larger degree of surface oxidation might cause more surface defects, leading to red-shifted emission (Mintz et al., 2019). The luminescence property KFP-CDs could be ascribed due to the presence of –C–N, –COOH, and –OH attached to the carbon surface (Atchudan et al., 2020; Jin et al., 2013).

The zeta potential measurement was conducted for the synthesized KFP-CDs to estimate the charge on the KFP-CDs surface. As shown in Figure S3, the obtained zeta potential of synthesized KFP-CDs is around −35 mV. The negative zeta potential value of KFP-CDs further supports our previous characterization results, the presence of oxygen/nitrogen-containing functional groups such as carbonyl, hydroxyls, and amino on the surface and or edges of the synthesized KFP-CDs. As discussed in before sections, the oxygen/nitrogen-containing functional groups are responsible for the high dispersibility/solubility of KFP-CDs in water or polar medium.

One of the most important characteristics for application areas is the fluorescence photostability of KFP-CDs. The photostability of fluorescent materials is critically relevant for their use in the biological field. The fluorometric method was performed to study the photostability of the KFP-CDs. Fig. 6 demonstrate the fluorescence spectra of KFP-CDs at various storage time and irradiation time. Fig. 6(a) shows the response of emission spectra in KFP-CDs with 360 nm UV exposure at various storage times from 1 to 90 days. It is worth mentioning that KFP-CDs stored for 90 days showed a relatively same intensity of emission spectrum compared to that of day 1. This observation confirms that KFP-CDs have excellent prolonged stability even after a longer storage time. In addition, the variation of UV irradiation time from 0 to 90 min upon KFP-CDs with the emission spectrum is shown in Fig. 6(b). The intensity of the emission spectrum maintained a constant value indicating a remarkable photostability of KFP-CDs at various irradiation times. Because of this characteristic, KFP-CDs make effective fluorescent probes for live-cell imaging, which might be useful in microscopy and analytical purposes.

KFP-CDs can be utilized as fluorescent ink because of their high dispersibility in aqueous media, strong fluorescence, photobleaching resistance, and optical transparency range (Qu et al., 2012). Fig. 7 shows the images of calligraphy using KFP-CDs ink illuminated under UV light. This was accomplished by filling a market available sketch pen with an aqueous solution of synthesized KFP-CDs and drawing on regular filter paper. Exposure of a 365 nm UV lamp onto the drawing resulted in a brilliant cyan blue fluorescence. These images reveal the significant application of KFP-CDs as fluorescent ink with extraordinary photostability. In order to make a comparison between the UV light and daylight exposure, the calligraphy using KFP-CDs ink was demonstrated as shown in Fig. 8 . Under daylight (Fig. 8(b)), the KFP-CDs aqueous solution was colorless, but under the UV lamp (Fig. 8(a)), bright blue fluorescence was produced. This observation confirms the existence of a glowing effect only with UV exposure. This suggests that the KFP-CDs might be exploited as invisible ink for purposes like confidential messaging and anti-counterfeiting (Liu et al., 2021). Besides, the hand-drawn image was kept for 4 months under atmospheric conditions and was exposed under UV light (365 nm) and normal lamp (daylight) to determine the stability of KFP-CDs. Both the photographs (drawn at the time of synthesis Fig. 8(a and b) and after 4 months Fig. 8(c and d)) are nearly similar which did not show any significant differences. This result suggests that the KFP-CDs exhibits stable fluorescence for a long time under static conditions (air atmosphere). This fluorescent ink was also biocompatible, durable, transparent, pollution-free, and easily washable, making it a viable alternative to regular fluorescent ink (Desai et al., 2019; Lu et al., 2015; Ma et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Normal and cancer cellular viability to synthesized KFP-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of synthesized KFP-CDs on the cellular viability of human normal and cancer cells were evaluated by performing a CCK8 assay. Human cell lines (BM-MSCs, MDA-MB-231, Cal62, and BHP10-3SCp) were treated with various concentrations (0, 25, 50, 75, 100, 125, 150, 175, and 200 μg mL−1) of the synthesized KFP-CDs for 24 and 48 h. The assay results revealed no change in the cell viability of BM-MSCs in the treatment group compared to the control group at 24 h all the concentrations and at 48 h cell viability decreased (*p < 0.05) at 175 and 200 μg mL−1 (Fig. 9 (a)). The assay results revealed no change in the cell viability of MDA-MB-231 and Cal62 in the treatment group compared to the control group at 24 h in all the concentrations and at 48 h cell viability decreased (*p < 0.05) at 200 μg mL−1 (Fig. 9(b and c)). The assay results revealed no change in the cell viability of BHP10-3SCp in the treatment group compared to the control group at 24 and 48 h in all the concentrations (Fig. 9(d)). Our results suggest that synthesized KFP-CDs are nontoxic to both normal and cancer cells (up to 150 μg mL−1), these results are in agreement with other studies (Atchudan et al., 2018b; Edison et al., 2016a; Li et al., 2020). Generally, the cancer cells show heterogeneity and are not susceptible to cytotoxicity, but whereas BM-MSCs show homogeneity and are susceptible to cytotoxicity but synthesized KFP-CDs were astonishingly non-toxic to BM-MSCs. BM-MSCs are used to treat various diseases (Rajendran et al., 2020) and cancers are used to make tumor models for experimenting with various therapies in mouse models (Oh et al., 2020) so this is critical for the safe and sustainable development of cellular imaging.

(a-d) The CCK8 assay results of BM-MSCs, MDA-MB-231, Cal62, and BHP10-3SCp cells were treated with the synthesized KFP-CDs (0, 25, 50, 75, 100, 125, 150, 175, and 200 μg mL−1) for 24 and 48 h. The data is represented in bar graph form as percentages (n = 4). The student's t-test was performed for statistical analysis. *p < 0.05 and “ns” is not significant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multi-color imaging of normal and cancer cells using synthesized KFP-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
To validate the potential application of synthesized KFP-CDs for live-cell imaging, KFP-CDs were used as an agent for laser confocal microscopy imaging of BM-MSCs, MDA-MB-231, Cal62, and BHP10-3SCp cells. After incubation of CDs for 24 h with BM-MSCs, MDA-MB-231, Cal62 and BHP10-3SCp cells at a concentration of 150 μg mL−1, All the cells with KFP-CDs showed blue, green, and red colors at the respective wavelength, which reveals an active internalization of KFP-CDs into all four cell types. In contrast, the cells without KFP-CDs showed no significant fluorescence signals. All three colors (blue, green, and red) were strong, and the merged image showed that KFP-CDs are distributed all over the cells, mainly in the cytoplasm of cells. In addition to that, all the cells showed signals of all three colors. Similar, results were observed at 48 h as well, where the brightness (intensity) of signals in all three colors were increased compared to 24 h (Figs. 10–13
). These results showed bright three color signals, KFP-CDs can be used to label cancer cells or BM-MSCs for visualizing in vitro interaction or migration in co-culture experiments (Oellers et al., 2009; Pijuan et al., 2019; Vis et al., 2020), which are crucial to understanding the changes in cellular migration and morphology. We believe that strong signals emitted from the KFP-CDs may be utilized for understanding the biodistribution and fate of cells in vivo in a short time, as MSCs are used for cell-based therapies in various diseases including cancers (Rajendran et al., 2020; Zhuang et al., 2021) and KFP-CDs labeled cancer cells can be used to establish a metastasis mouse models (Elkin and Vlodavsky, 2001; Rashid et al., 2013). The results suggest that the KFP-CDs were capable of being used as a cell labeling agent for both in vitro and in vivo imaging for BM-MSCs, breast cancer cells, and thyroid cancer cells.

In conclusion, using kiwi fruit peels waste as a precursor for carbon, KFP-CDs were synthesized using a simple hydrothermal carbonization technique in an environmentally acceptable way. The KFP-CDs had a homogenous spherical shape, even size distribution, and an average size of 5 nm with a moderate degree of graphitization making them highly dispersible in aqueous environments. They were also attached with functional groups such as –C–N, –COOH, and –O–H enabling the strong fluorescence without any pretreatment, and displayed excitation-dependent fluorescence emission with an acceptable QY of 18%. KFP-CDs proved to display an excellent photostability, maintaining consistency in the intensity of fluorescence emission spectrum for samples irradiated for 90 min. KFP-CDs exploited as fluorescent ink was biocompatible, everlasting, transparent, pollution-free, and easily washable. Our results suggest that synthesized KFP-CDs are nontoxic to both normal and cancer cells (up to 150 μg mL−1), which is essential for the safe and sustainable development of cellular imaging. Also, the KFP-CDs were capable of being used as a cell labeling agent for both in vitro and in vivo imaging for MSCs, breast cancer cells, and thyroid cancer cells. In this context, our investigation has achieved a tremendous pathway for the conversion of biowaste into an environmentally friendly material for biomedical application and paves the path for the future development of biowaste materials into pollution-free innovative products and applications.
